work_and_condition_changes_since_reconsi.describe_the_nature_and_extent_of_work:
Since filing my request for reconsideration, I have only attempted very limited, part-time work on two short occasions, and I was not able to sustain either attempt. From 2024-03-11 to 2024-04-05, I worked approximately 6–8 hours per week as a remote customer service support assistant for a small online craft business owned by a family friend. My duties were limited to answering a few emails and occasionally responding to simple customer questions using pre-written templates. I worked from home at my kitchen table using my laptop. I was allowed to choose my own hours in small blocks of 30–45 minutes because I could not sit for longer without significant back pain, leg numbness, and increased migraines.

Even with this flexibility, I had to lie down multiple times during what should have been a short shift and often could not complete the hours I was scheduled for. During that month, my symptoms (chronic low back pain, sciatica down my left leg, and migraines) increased significantly. I frequently had to cancel or cut short my work periods. My employer informally agreed to let me go because I was unable to meet even the minimal expectations. I earned less than $250 total during that time.

Later, from 2024-07-08 to 2024-07-19, I tried a very short trial of part-time work (about 4 hours per week) as a receptionist helper at my church office. This was unpaid volunteer-type work, but they intended to pay me a small stipend. I was supposed to sit at the front desk, answer the phone, and greet visitors. After the first week, I had a severe flare-up of back pain and numbness, and I had to leave early both times I came in. During the second week, I missed one scheduled shift completely due to a migraine and had to leave after only 45 minutes on another day. After discussing it with the church administrator, we agreed that I could not reliably perform even this very light and flexible work.

Since then, I have not attempted any further work because each attempt has resulted in worsening pain, increased use of medication, and several days in bed recovering. I am not earning any income from work and remain unable to perform any substantial gainful activity on a consistent basis.
work_and_condition_changes_since_reconsi.describe_the_change_in_your_condition:
Since the date of my request for reconsideration, my overall condition has gradually worsened, both in terms of pain severity and how often I experience flare-ups. My primary issues are chronic lumbar degenerative disc disease with a herniated disc at L4-L5, left-sided sciatica, and chronic migraine headaches. I also experience anxiety and depression related to my ongoing pain and loss of ability to work.

Back and leg pain: The sharp, shooting pain that travels from my lower back down my left leg into my calf and sometimes my foot has become more constant. Previously, I had some days where the pain was more of a dull ache, but now I have radiating, burning pain on most days. Numbness and tingling in my left foot and toes last longer and come on more quickly when I sit or stand. My doctor noted decreased strength and reflexes on the left side at my last exam. The episodes where my leg "gives out" have increased from once every couple of weeks to at least once or twice a week, which has made me more fearful of falling.

Sitting, standing, and walking tolerance: My ability to sit, stand, or walk has decreased. I used to be able to sit in a regular chair for about 30–40 minutes before needing to change positions; now I can usually only tolerate about 15–20 minutes before the pain starts to spike. Standing in one place (such as at the sink or stove) is very difficult after about 10 minutes, and I often have to lean on the counter or sit down. Walking even a short distance, like from the parking lot into the grocery store, can cause a flare-up of pain that lasts the rest of the day. I now need to use a heating pad and lie flat several times a day to calm the pain.

Migraines: My migraine headaches have also increased in frequency and intensity. Before, I had about 4–5 migraines a month; now I average 7–10 per month. During a migraine, I have severe throbbing pain, nausea, and extreme sensitivity to light and noise. I usually must lie in a dark, quiet room with an ice pack on my head. These can last anywhere from 6 hours to more than a full day. My neurologist has had to adjust my medications, and I have had to go to urgent care twice for injections when the pain would not respond to my usual treatments.

Mental health: Emotionally, I feel more anxious and depressed. I worry constantly about finances and about being a burden on my family. I have more trouble concentrating and remembering things, which my doctors think is partly due to chronic pain and partly due to the medication side effects. I sometimes feel overwhelmed by simple tasks like paying bills or planning meals, and I cry more easily than I used to. My primary care doctor referred me for counseling, which I have started, but I still struggle with low motivation and fatigue.

Sleep and fatigue: My sleep has gotten worse. I wake up multiple times during the night because of back pain or leg cramps. I rarely feel rested in the morning. As a result, I am more fatigued during the day and sometimes need to nap. This makes it even harder to maintain any kind of schedule.

Overall, the combination of increased pain, reduced physical tolerance, more frequent migraines, and worsening emotional health has made it even more difficult for me to perform daily tasks or consider returning to any kind of regular work, even on a part-time or flexible basis.
work_and_condition_changes_since_reconsi.describe_the_changes_in_daily_activities:
Yes, my daily activities and social functioning have changed noticeably since the date of my reconsideration request, mostly in a negative way.

Daily activities: I used to be able to manage most household chores myself, just at a slower pace. Now I can only do light tasks and often need help. For example, I used to be able to cook simple dinners several nights a week. Now I can usually only manage very basic meals that require minimal standing, like making sandwiches or heating something in the microwave. Standing at the stove to cook anything that requires chopping or stirring for more than about 10 minutes causes my lower back to tighten and the pain to radiate down my leg. On bad days, my teenage daughter or my husband prepares dinner, or we eat frozen meals.

Laundry and cleaning: Lifting laundry baskets up and down the stairs is no longer possible for me because of the strain on my back. My husband now carries the baskets, and I might fold a few items while sitting on the couch, but I often have to stop and lie down partway through. Vacuuming is very difficult; the pushing and pulling motion aggravates my back and shoulder, so I rarely do more than a small area at a time, and my family handles the rest. I also have trouble bending to clean the bathtub or pick things up off the floor, so the house is not as tidy as it used to be.

Personal care: I can still bathe and dress myself, but it takes longer and is more painful. I have to be very careful when stepping into and out of the shower, and I use a shower chair on days when my leg feels weak. Putting on socks and shoes can be challenging because bending forward hurts my lower back, so I sometimes sit on the edge of the bed and take several breaks while getting dressed. On days with severe pain or a migraine, I may stay in pajamas until the afternoon.

Errands and driving: I drive much less than I used to. Sitting in the car for more than 15–20 minutes significantly increases my pain, and turning my head quickly can trigger or worsen a migraine. I now limit myself to short trips close to home, like a quick run to the pharmacy or the small grocery store around the corner, and even that often requires me to rest afterward. My husband or sister now handles most of the major grocery shopping and any longer drives, such as taking our daughter to activities or doctor appointments that are farther away.

Social activities: I have had to cut back on social activities. Before, I regularly attended church services and small group meetings and occasionally went out to lunch or coffee with friends. Now I often miss church because sitting on the pews for the full service is too painful, and the lighting and noise can trigger migraines. When I do attend, I usually sit in the back so I can stand or shift positions, and I often leave early. I rarely accept invitations to social gatherings because I cannot predict when I will have a pain flare or migraine. I have canceled on friends multiple times at the last minute, which makes me feel guilty and has caused me to withdraw even more.

Family life: At home, I am less able to participate in family activities. I used to take walks around the neighborhood with my husband and daughter in the evenings; now I can only walk a very short distance and often have to turn back early or not go at all. When my family watches movies together, I frequently have to lie on the couch with pillows under my legs, and sometimes I have to go to the bedroom because I cannot tolerate sitting or the sound from the TV if I feel a migraine coming on. I feel like I miss out on important moments because of my pain.

Emotional and social functioning: I have become more isolated and withdrawn. I feel embarrassed needing to explain my limitations, and I worry people think I am exaggerating. I sometimes avoid phone calls and texts because I am too tired or in too much pain to talk. I also struggle with concentration, so following long conversations or remembering details later can be difficult. This has affected my confidence and made me reluctant to meet new people or participate in group activities. Overall, my world has gotten much smaller, and I rely heavily on my immediate family for support and help with basic tasks.

recent_medical_treatment.address_include_zip_code: Evergreen Family Medicine Clinic, 2145 Willow Creek Rd, Suite 300, Portland, OR 97225
recent_medical_treatment.how_often_do_you_see_this_physician: Approximately every 2–3 months for follow-up visits, plus occasional urgent appointments during severe flare-ups.
recent_medical_treatment.reason_for_visit:
Routine follow-up for chronic low back pain with left-sided sciatica, medication management, review of imaging results, and evaluation of worsening symptoms. On 2024-02-19, I saw Dr. McKenzie because my back pain had increased, and I was having more frequent episodes of numbness and tingling in my left leg and foot. I also reported that I was having difficulty sleeping due to pain and that over-the-counter medications were no longer helping much.

On 2024-04-29, I returned because my attempt at part-time remote work had caused a significant flare in my symptoms. I described increased pain after sitting for more than 20 minutes, more frequent leg weakness, and a few near-falls. I also discussed increased feelings of anxiety and sadness about my limitations.

On 2024-07-01, I had a follow-up to review the results of a recent MRI and to discuss ongoing pain that was not adequately controlled by my current medication regimen. I reported that my daily functioning had declined, that I was relying more on my family for basic chores, and that my migraines were also becoming more frequent.

On 2024-09-16, I saw him again because my pain and functional limitations had continued to worsen. I reported more days spent lying down, increased difficulty with standing and walking, and more frequent migraines, as well as ongoing emotional distress related to my chronic condition and inability to work.
recent_medical_treatment.type_of_treatment_received_include_drugs:
At Evergreen Family Medicine Clinic, Dr. McKenzie has provided ongoing conservative treatment and management of my chronic conditions. This has included:

1. Medications:
- Prescription of naproxen 500 mg twice daily as needed for pain and inflammation. I was instructed to take it with food and to monitor for stomach upset.
- Prescription of gabapentin 300 mg at bedtime, later increased to 300 mg twice daily, to help with nerve pain and the burning, tingling sensations in my left leg and foot.
- Occasional short courses of muscle relaxants (cyclobenzaprine 5–10 mg at night) during acute flare-ups when my back muscles go into spasm.
- Adjustment of my existing migraine medication regimen, including continuing sumatriptan 50 mg as needed at the onset of a migraine and adding a low-dose beta blocker (propranolol 20 mg twice daily) as a preventive medication.
- Prescription of an antidepressant (sertraline 50 mg daily) to help with anxiety and depression related to chronic pain and disability.

2. Imaging and tests:
- Ordering of a lumbar spine MRI (performed at an imaging center on 2024-05-20) which confirmed degenerative disc disease, a herniated disc at L4-L5 impinging on the nerve root, and mild spinal stenosis.
- Periodic neurological examinations in the office, including checking my reflexes, strength, and sensation in both legs, which showed decreased sensation and slightly reduced strength on the left side.

3. Referrals and non-medication treatments:
- Referral to physical therapy focusing on gentle core strengthening, stretching, and education on body mechanics. I attended sessions twice a week for about 6 weeks, but I had to stop when my pain worsened, and my therapist and doctor agreed that we should scale back.
- Recommendation for the use of a lumbar support brace during certain activities and instruction in home exercises I can do lying down with minimal strain.
- Discussion of possible pain management options, including epidural steroid injections, and referral to a pain management specialist (appointment pending at the time of my last visit).
- Referral to counseling/behavioral health services for support in coping with chronic pain, depression, and anxiety.

4. Lifestyle and activity modifications:
- Detailed instructions on activity pacing, including alternating short periods of sitting, standing, and lying down.
- Guidance to avoid heavy lifting, bending, and twisting motions and to use proper posture and supportive seating.
- Advice to keep a pain and migraine diary to track triggers, frequency, and response to medications.

Overall, the treatment has been focused on managing symptoms and trying to maintain as much function as possible, but despite these efforts, my pain levels and functional limitations have remained significant and, in some ways, have worsened over time.
